knowledge.what_it_is: Objective-C is a general-purpose, object-oriented programming language developed by Apple Inc., used primarily for developing iOS and macOS applications.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in Objective-C, including types (int, float, double) and syntax
Data Types — understanding primitive data types (int, char, float), pointers, and structs in Objective-C
Operators — mastering arithmetic, assignment, comparison, logical, and bitwise operators in Objective-C
Control Structures — learning if-else statements, switch statements, loops (for, while, do-while) in Objective-C
Functions — declaring and using functions in Objective-C, including function arguments and return types
Arrays and Vectors — working with NSArray, NSMutableArray, NSAArray, and NSVector in Objective-C
Dictionaries — understanding NSDictionary, NSMutableDictionary, and NSMapTable in Objective-C
Blocks — learning block syntax, capture lists, and using blocks as function arguments or return values

knowledge.what_it_is: Interoperability between Objective-C and Swift allows iOS developers to seamlessly integrate code written in both languages, enabling the creation of complex and feature-rich apps. This is achieved through a combination of language features, frameworks, and tools.
what_you_will_learn: Protocol-oriented programming — defining protocols that can be adopted by classes or structs in either language
Objective-C to Swift bridging — using the @objc attribute to expose Objective-C classes and methods to Swift
Swift to Objective-C bridging — using the @objc attribute to expose Swift classes and methods to Objective-C
Protocol conformance — making a class or struct conform to an Objective-C protocol in Swift
CocoaPods integration — using CocoaPods to manage dependencies and integrate Objective-C libraries into Swift projects
SwiftUI interoperability — using SwiftUI views and controls within an Objective-C app, and vice versa
Core Data integration — integrating Core Data models with Swift code, including data modeling and fetching
NSManagedObject subclassing — creating custom NSManagedObject subclasses in Swift to interact with Core Data

knowledge.what_it_is: Swift is a high-performance, modern programming language developed by Apple for building iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS apps. It's designed to be easy to learn and use, with a clean syntax and powerful features.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and Data Types — declaring and using variables, understanding value types (e.g., Int, String) and reference types (e.g., Array, Dictionary)
Control Flow Statements — if-else statements, switch statements, loops (for-in, while), and conditional statements
Functions — defining and calling functions, function parameters, return types, and closures
Classes and Objects — creating classes, inheritance, polymorphism, and object-oriented programming concepts
Protocols — defining protocols, implementing protocol methods, and using protocol extensions
Optionals — working with optional values, unwrapping optionals, and using nil-coalescing operator
Closures — capturing variables in closures, escaping closures, and using trailing closure syntax
Error Handling — throwing and catching errors, error types (e.g., Error, NSError), and custom error handling
Generics — defining generic types, constraints, and using generic functions
Enumerations — creating enumerations, cases, and associated values
Structs and Classes — understanding the difference between structs and classes, value semantics, and reference semantics
Concurrency — working with Grand Central Dispatch (GCD), threads, and concurrency patterns
Networking — using URLSession for networking, data tasks, and downloading files
Persistence — storing data using Core Data, Realm, or SQLite
UI Programming — creating UI components, views, and view controllers
Storyboarding — designing user interfaces in Interface Builder, segues, and navigation controllers

knowledge.what_it_is: Swift is a modern, high-performance, and safe programming language developed by Apple for building iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS apps. It was first introduced at WWDC 2014 as a replacement for Objective-C, with the goal of making app development easier, faster, and more enjoyable.
what_you_will_learn: Objective-C vs Swift: Key differences in syntax, performance, and safety features
History of Swift: From its initial release to the current version (Swift 5.7) and its adoption by Apple
Why choose Swift for iOS development: Performance, memory management, and error handling advantages
Swift's type system: Optional binding, generics, and protocols for safer code
Cocoa and Cocoa Touch frameworks: Understanding their role in building iOS apps with Swift
Xcode integration: Using the latest Xcode features to write, debug, and test Swift code

knowledge.what_it_is: Objective-C was Apple's primary language for iOS development until Swift took over as the recommended choice. Although still supported, it has several limitations and drawbacks compared to Swift.
what_you_will_learn: Memory Management — Understanding ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) vs MRC (Manual Reference Counting), and why Swift's ARC is a game-changer
Error Handling — How Objective-C's NSError vs Swift's Error types differ, and the benefits of using try-catch blocks in Swift
Type Safety — Why Swift's type system is more robust than Objective-C's, including features like Optional Binding and Pattern Matching
Nullability — Understanding the difference between nullable and non-nullable types in Objective-C vs Swift, and why Swift's non-nullability is safer
CocoaPods Integration — How to integrate CocoaPods with your project, including podfile configuration and dependency management
Swift Interoperability — How to call Swift code from Objective-C and vice versa, including the use of bridging headers and modules
Code Readability — Why Swift's syntax is more concise and expressive than Objective-C's, including features like Tuples and Extensions

knowledge.what_it_is: Swift is Apple's modern, high-performance programming language for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS app development. It's designed to give developers more freedom and expressiveness while ensuring the reliability and efficiency of their apps.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and Data Types — declaring and using variables with let and var, understanding value types (Int, String) and reference types (Array, Dictionary)
Control Flow Statements — if-else statements, switch statements, loops (for-in, while), and control flow operators (guard, fallthrough)
Functions — defining and calling functions, function parameters and return types, and function overloading
Arrays and Collections — working with arrays, sets, dictionaries, and other collection types in Swift
Optionals — understanding the concept of optionals, unwrapping optionals using if-let, guard, and optional binding
Closures — defining and using closures, capturing values in a closure, and escaping closures
Error Handling — throwing and catching errors using do-catch blocks, error types (Error, NSError), and custom error handling
Protocols and Extensions — defining protocols, conforming to protocols, and extending existing types with new functionality

knowledge.what_it_is: Cocoa Touch is a software framework developed by Apple for building iOS and iPadOS applications. It provides a set of libraries, tools, and APIs that enable developers to create user interfaces, handle events, and interact with hardware components.
what_you_will_learn: UIView — the root view class in Cocoa Touch, responsible for rendering visual content
UIViewController — manages the life cycle of views, handles navigation, and provides a container for child views
UITableView and UICollectionView — data-driven UI components for displaying lists and grids of data
UINavigationController — navigates between view controllers using a stack-based approach
Segue — a transition mechanism that enables passing data between view controllers
Storyboard and XIB files — design-time tools for creating and managing user interfaces
Auto Layout — a system for dynamically arranging views based on constraints and priorities
Constraints with anchors, margins, and padding — defining the position and size of views within their superviews

knowledge.what_it_is: The Core OS framework provides a set of services and APIs that enable iOS, iPadOS, watchOS, and tvOS devices to manage low-level system resources such as memory, storage, and power. It is responsible for providing a stable and secure foundation for the operating system.
what_you_will_learn: Memory Management — understanding how Core OS manages memory on iOS devices using ARC (Automatic Reference Counting), MRC (Manual Reference Counting), and memory debugging tools like Xcode's Memory Graph Debugger
Process Management — learning about process creation, termination, and synchronization in Core OS, including the use of pthreads and dispatch queues
Thread Synchronization — understanding how to synchronize access to shared resources using locks, semaphores, and other synchronization primitives in Core OS
Power Management — learning about power management techniques such as low-power modes, wake-up events, and power-efficient design patterns in Core OS
Storage Management — understanding how Core OS manages storage on iOS devices, including the use of file systems, storage classes, and caching mechanisms
Security Frameworks — learning about security frameworks like Security.framework, which provides APIs for data protection, encryption, and secure coding practices in Core OS

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Services are a set of frameworks and libraries provided by Apple to simplify common tasks on iOS, including networking, file management, and data storage. They provide a unified API for accessing various system resources.
what_you_will_learn: URLSession — the modern replacement for NSURLConnection, providing a high-level interface for making HTTP requests
URLSessionTask — a subclass of URLSession that provides more fine-grained control over task management and caching
FileProvider — a framework for sharing files between apps and services on iOS, including iCloud integration
CloudKit — a suite of APIs for storing and retrieving data in the cloud, including user authentication and authorization
Core Data — a powerful object-graph management system that provides a simple way to manage model objects and relationships
Keychain Access — a framework for securely storing sensitive data such as passwords and encryption keys
Security Framework — a set of APIs for encrypting and decrypting data, including support for common cryptographic algorithms

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Audio is Apple's low-level, high-performance audio framework for iOS and macOS development. It provides a comprehensive set of APIs for handling audio playback, recording, and processing.
what_you_will_learn: AVAudioEngine — a high-level API for playing and recording audio, providing a simple and efficient way to manage audio sessions
AVPlayerItem — a subclass of AVPlayer that allows for more fine-grained control over audio playback, including the ability to seek and scrub through content
AVAudioSession — a singleton class responsible for managing the audio session, including setting up input and output routes, configuring audio formats, and handling interruptions
Audio Units — a low-level API for processing audio in real-time, providing access to a wide range of built-in effects and filters
Core Audio Pipeline — a framework for building custom audio pipelines, allowing developers to create complex audio processing chains using a combination of Audio Units and other Core Audio APIs
AVAudioPCMBuffer — a class used for representing raw audio data in memory, providing an efficient way to transfer audio data between different parts of the app
AVAudioFormat — a class used for specifying the format of audio data, including parameters such as sample rate, bit depth, and channel count

knowledge.what_it_is: Metal is Apple's low-overhead, high-performance graphics and compute API for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS. It allows developers to harness the power of the GPU for tasks like rendering, physics, and machine learning.
what_you_will_learn: Buffers — creating vertex buffers, index buffers, and uniform buffers for efficient data transfer
Textures — loading and binding textures for use in shaders, including texture sampling and filtering
Shaders — writing compute and graphics shaders using Metal Shading Language (MSL), including vertex and fragment shaders
Render Passes — creating render passes to manage the rendering pipeline, including setting up command encoders and executing commands
Command Buffers — using command buffers for asynchronous execution of commands, including recording and submitting commands
Compute Pipelines — creating compute pipelines for general-purpose computing tasks, including data processing and machine learning
Buffers vs. Textures — understanding the differences between buffers and textures in Metal, and when to use each
Metal Performance Shaders (MPS) — using MPS for optimized implementation of common graphics algorithms, including convolution and filtering

knowledge.what_it_is: Media is a framework in iOS that provides a unified way to handle multimedia content, including audio and video playback, image processing, and more. It is designed to work seamlessly with other Apple frameworks such as AVFoundation and Core Image.
what_you_will_learn: AVPlayer — a high-performance media player for playing videos, audios, and live streams
AVAsset — a container object that holds metadata and tracks of a media asset, including audio and video
AVComposition — a composition object that allows you to combine multiple media assets into a single file
AVPlayerItem — an item that represents a media asset in the AVPlayer, providing access to its metadata and tracks
AVCaptureSession — a session that manages the capture of video and audio from a device's camera and microphone
AVAssetTrack — a track object that holds the metadata and data for a specific type of media (e.g. audio or video)
AVCompositionTrack — a track object that represents a composition of multiple media assets
AVPlayerLooper — a looper that allows you to play a media asset in a loop, with options for repeat and shuffle

knowledge.what_it_is: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm used to design and implement classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and abstraction. In iOS development, OOP principles are applied using Objective-C or Swift languages.
what_you_will_learn: Classes — defining custom classes with properties, methods, and initializers
Objects — creating instances of classes with attributes and behaviors
Inheritance — subclassing to inherit properties and behavior from parent classes
Polymorphism — using method overriding and overloading for generic code reuse
Encapsulation — hiding internal implementation details within objects or classes
Abstraction — exposing only necessary information to the outside world, while hiding complexity
Properties — declaring instance variables with getter and setter methods in Swift
Protocols — defining interfaces that can be adopted by multiple classes for polymorphism

knowledge.what_it_is: Functional programming is a paradigm that emphasizes the use of pure functions, immutability, and recursion to compose software. In iOS development, functional programming is used extensively with Swift's built-in support for higher-order functions, closures, and protocols.
what_you_will_learn: Pure functions — writing functions that always return the same output given the same inputs, without side effects
Immutability — using structs and classes to create immutable data structures, reducing bugs caused by unintended mutations
Map, Filter, Reduce — applying these higher-order functions to collections for efficient data processing
Closures — capturing variables in a function's scope to create reusable code blocks
Higher-Order Functions (HOFs) — using functions as arguments or return values to abstract away complex logic
Recursion — breaking down problems into smaller sub-problems, solving them recursively with Swift's tail call optimization
Functional data structures — using Swift's built-in support for immutable arrays and dictionaries to create thread-safe collections

knowledge.what_it_is: Memory management is a critical aspect of iOS app development, ensuring that your app uses memory efficiently to prevent crashes and optimize performance.
what_you_will_learn: ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) — automatically manages memory for objects by tracking retain counts
Strong vs Weak References — understanding the difference between strong and weak references in ARC
Memory Leaks — identifying and fixing memory leaks caused by retained cycles or zombie objects
Manual Memory Management with MRC (Manual Reference Counting) — using manual reference counting to manage memory for legacy code or performance-critical sections
ARC vs MRC — comparing the benefits and trade-offs of Automatic Reference Counting and Manual Reference Counting
Memory Profiling with Instruments — using Xcode's Instruments tool to detect memory leaks and optimize app performance
Core Data Memory Management — understanding how Core Data manages memory for objects and relationships
Swift Memory Safety Features — leveraging Swift's built-in memory safety features, such as ARC and Optional binding

knowledge.what_it_is: The ViewController lifecycle refers to the sequence of events that occur when a view controller is created, loaded into memory, displayed on screen, and eventually dismissed or destroyed. In iOS development, understanding the ViewController lifecycle is crucial for managing app state, handling user interactions, and optimizing performance.
what_you_will_learn: View Controller Creation — understanding how to create a new instance of a view controller using Storyboard or programmatically
loadView() vs loadViewFromNib() — knowing when to use each method for loading the view hierarchy
viewDidLoad() — understanding what code should be executed here, such as setting up UI components and handling initial layout
viewWillAppear() vs viewWillAppear(animated:) — learning the differences between these two methods for handling view appearance
viewDidAppear() — knowing how to execute code when the view is fully visible on screen
viewWillDisappear() — understanding what code should be executed here, such as saving app state and releasing resources
viewDidDisappear() — knowing how to handle view disappearance events
dealloc() — understanding the role of this method in releasing memory and cleaning up resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Error handling is a crucial aspect of iOS development, ensuring that your app remains stable and provides a good user experience even when unexpected errors occur.
what_you_will_learn: Try-Catch Blocks — using Swift's built-in try-catch syntax to catch and handle errors in a clean and readable way
Error Types — understanding the different types of errors in iOS, including NSError, Error, and custom error classes
Error Handling in Async Code — handling errors that occur in asynchronous code, such as with URLSession or DispatchQueue
Custom Error Classes — creating custom error classes to provide more context and information about specific errors
Error Propagation — understanding how errors propagate through your app's call stack and how to handle them effectively
Unwind Segues and Exception Handling — handling exceptions that occur during unwind segues, such as when a user navigates back in the navigation controller
Swift Error Handling with do-try-catch — using Swift's do-try-catch syntax to simplify error handling and reduce boilerplate code

knowledge.what_it_is: Concurrency refers to the ability of an iOS app to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, improving responsiveness and user experience. Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) and async/await are two key concurrency frameworks used in iOS development.
what_you_will_learn: GCD Queues — creating concurrent queues for executing tasks asynchronously, with options for serial or concurrent execution
Dispatch Groups — synchronizing tasks across multiple queues using dispatch groups
async/await — writing asynchronous code that's easier to read and maintain, using the async keyword and await expression
Swift Concurrency API — using the new concurrency API in Swift 5.5 and later for asynchronous programming
OperationQueue — executing tasks asynchronously on a queue with support for dependencies and cancellation
Dispatch Semaphore — synchronizing access to shared resources using dispatch semaphores
Completable Futures — handling asynchronous results using completable futures
Task Groups — executing multiple tasks concurrently using task groups

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Graphics is Apple's 2D and 3D graphics API, used to create custom visual effects, animations, and user interfaces on iOS devices.
what_you_will_learn: CGContext — a graphics context that encapsulates the drawing operations, including paths, images, and text
CGPath — a path object used for creating complex shapes and curves, with methods like addLineToPoint() and moveToPoint()
CALayer — a layer class that manages its own content, layout, and animation, often used in combination with Core Graphics
Core Image filters — a set of built-in image processing filters, including color correction, distortion, and texture effects
CGAffineTransform — a 2D affine transformation matrix for rotating, scaling, and translating graphics
UIBezierPath — a path object that can be used to create complex shapes and curves, with methods like addArcWithCenter() and closePath()
CATransform3D — a 3D transformation matrix for rotating, scaling, and translating graphics in 3D space

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Image is a powerful image processing framework provided by Apple, allowing developers to perform various operations on images such as filtering, transforming, and compositing. It is widely used in iOS apps for tasks like photo editing, effects, and visualizations.
what_you_will_learn: Image Processing Pipeline — understanding the sequence of operations performed on an image, including input, processing, and output
CIImage and CIContext — working with Core Image's fundamental classes for creating and manipulating images
Filters and Effects — applying built-in filters like blur, sharpen, and color adjustments, as well as custom effects using CIFilter
Color Space Conversion — converting between different color spaces (e.g., RGB, CMYK, sRGB) and color models (e.g., HSL, HSV)
Image Transformations — scaling, rotating, flipping, and cropping images using CIImage's methods
Compositing — combining multiple images or layers into a single image using CICategoryCompositing
Color Management — working with color profiles, ICC profiles, and color spaces for accurate color representation
Performance Optimization — techniques for improving Core Image performance, including caching, batching, and multithreading

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, widely used by iOS developers to manage their code repositories, track changes, and collaborate with team members.
what_you_will_learn: Creating a new repository — initializing a Git repository in Xcode, pushing local commits to GitHub
Branching and merging — using Git branches for feature development, testing, and deployment, merging changes into the main branch
Pull requests — creating pull requests to review and merge code changes from team members or collaborators
Code reviews — participating in code reviews to ensure high-quality code, following Apple's coding standards and best practices
Release management — using GitHub releases to manage versioning, changelogs, and deployment of iOS apps
Dependency management — using CocoaPods or Carthage with GitHub repositories for dependency management and integration
API documentation — using GitHub Pages or Jekyll to generate API documentation for iOS projects

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a distributed version control system used by iOS developers to manage code changes, collaborate with team members, and track project history.
what_you_will_learn: git add — staging files for commit, can be used with --all option
git commit — creating snapshots of code changes, including commit messages and author information
git branch — managing different development branches, such as feature or release branches
git merge — combining changes from other branches into the current branch
git push — uploading local repository to a remote server, such as GitHub or GitLab
git pull — fetching changes from a remote server and merging them with the local repository
git log — viewing commit history, including commit messages and author information
git diff — comparing differences between files or commits

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, interfaces refer to the visual elements and interactions that users experience when using an app. They are created using Xcode's Interface Builder or programmatically using code.
what_you_will_learn: Storyboard Segues — connecting view controllers in a storyboard to navigate between screens
Segue Types (Show, Present, Modal) — understanding the different types of segues and when to use them
Auto Layout Constraints — creating constraints to position and size UI elements in a flexible way
Stack Views — using stack views to arrange and layout multiple UI elements
Table Views — displaying data in a table format with sections, headers, and cells
Collection Views — displaying data in a grid or collection format with cells and layouts

knowledge.what_it_is: Declarative syntax in iOS development refers to writing code that describes what should be done, rather than how it's done. This approach emphasizes the use of high-level abstractions and APIs provided by Apple's frameworks, making it easier to write efficient, readable, and maintainable code.
what_you_will_learn: SwiftUI — a declarative UI framework that allows you to describe your app's user interface using a concise, high-level syntax
Views — the building blocks of SwiftUI, used to create and compose UI elements
@ViewBuilder — a modifier that enables the creation of custom views with complex layouts
Binding — a mechanism for creating two-way data flow between views and view models
Published properties — a way to expose values from a view model to its associated views
EnvironmentObject — a way to share data between views using a centralized, observable store

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, components are reusable pieces of code that represent user interface elements or business logic. They can be thought of as building blocks that combine to form a complete app.
what_you_will_learn: View Controllers — the primary entry point for an app's UI, responsible for managing views and handling user interactions
Views — customizable UI components that display data and respond to user input, can be subclassed or created programmatically
Table Views — a type of view that displays a list of items in a table format, often used for displaying large datasets
Collection Views — similar to Table Views but designed for more complex layouts, such as grids or carousels
Stack Views — a simple way to stack views on top of each other, useful for creating basic layouts
Constraints and Auto Layout — the system that determines how views are laid out within their superviews, including constraints and anchors
Storyboard Segues — a way to transition between view controllers in a storyboard, can be used to navigate through an app's UI
Segue Types — understanding the different types of segues available, such as push, modal, and present

knowledge.what_it_is: Xcode is an integrated development environment (IDE) used to develop, test, and debug iOS apps. It's a crucial tool for any iOS developer.
what_you_will_learn: Downloading and installing Xcode from the Mac App Store — ensuring you have the latest version of Xcode
Setting up a new project in Xcode using the File > New > Project menu option — selecting a template, choosing a language (Swift or Objective-C), and setting up the project structure
Creating a new workspace in Xcode by navigating to File > New > Workspace — organizing multiple projects within a single workspace
Configuring Xcode's build settings for an iOS app, including setting the target device family, base SDK, and deployment target — ensuring your app is compatible with various devices and iOS versions
Using the Xcode Organizer to manage and transfer files between your Mac and connected iOS devices — facilitating testing and debugging on physical devices

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS, Views are the building blocks of user interfaces, responsible for rendering visual content on the screen. They can be thought of as containers that hold other views or controls.
what_you_will_learn: UIView — a basic view class that serves as the foundation for most custom views
UIViewController — a view controller class that manages a view and its associated data, responsible for handling user interactions and presenting views on screen
Storyboard Segues — a way to transition between views in an iOS app using storyboards, allowing for easy navigation between screens
Auto Layout Constraints — rules used to position and size views within their superviews, ensuring proper layout and responsiveness across different devices and orientations
Constraint-Based Layout — a system that uses auto layout constraints to arrange views in a flexible and adaptable way, ideal for complex layouts and dynamic content
View Hierarchy — the organizational structure of views in an iOS app, with each view having a parent-child relationship with other views
View Controller Lifecycle Methods — methods called at different stages of a view controller's life cycle, such as viewDidLoad(), viewWillAppear(), and viewWillDisappear()
Custom View Classes — creating custom classes that inherit from UIView or other view classes to create reusable and customizable views

knowledge.what_it_is: Xcode Preferences is a centralized location to manage various settings and configurations for your iOS development project, including code editing, debugging, and testing preferences.
what_you_will_learn: Code Editing — configuring syntax highlighting, font sizes, and line numbers in Xcode
Debugging — setting up breakpoints, console logging, and debug navigator options
Testing — managing test plans, test cases, and code coverage settings
Signing — configuring signing identities, certificates, and provisioning profiles for app distribution
Build Settings — customizing build configurations, compiler flags, and optimization levels
Scheme Management — creating, editing, and duplicating schemes for different development environments
Source Control Integration — setting up Git repositories, branch management, and commit messages

knowledge.what_it_is: A view controller is an iOS class that manages a view hierarchy and handles user interactions. It is responsible for presenting, managing, and dismissing views on screen.
what_you_will_learn: ViewController lifecycle — understanding the methods called at different stages of a view controller's life cycle (init, viewDidLoad, viewWillAppear, viewDidAppear)
Storyboard Segues — creating connections between view controllers using storyboards
Segue types (show, modal, push) — understanding how to use segues for different navigation scenarios
Navigation Controller — integrating a navigation controller into an app and configuring its properties
Tab Bar Controllers — implementing tab bar controllers with multiple tabs and views
Split View Controllers — handling iPad-specific split view controllers with master-detail layouts
Presenting/dismissing view controllers — understanding how to present and dismiss view controllers programmatically or using segues
View Controller containment — creating complex view hierarchies by embedding one view controller within another

knowledge.what_it_is: Creating a new project in Xcode is the starting point for any iOS development task, allowing you to set up a clean and organized workspace for your app.
what_you_will_learn: Project templates — selecting from pre-configured templates for different types of apps (e.g. Single View App, Tabbed App)
Target settings — configuring build settings, such as architecture, base SDK, and deployment target
Scheme management — creating and managing schemes to run, debug, or archive your app
Workspace organization — setting up folders, groups, and files to keep your project structured and easy to navigate
Swift Package Manager (SPM) setup — integrating third-party libraries using SPM
CocoaPods installation — adding dependencies using the popular package manager for iOS projects

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, an interface refers to the visual representation of your app's user experience, including views, controls, and layout. It's built using UIKit or SwiftUI frameworks.
what_you_will_learn: Storyboard vs XIB files — understanding the difference between these two file types for designing interfaces
ViewController lifecycle methods — learning about viewDidLoad(), viewDidAppear(), and other essential methods for managing interface state
Auto Layout basics — mastering constraints, anchors, and stack views to create flexible layouts
View hierarchy management — understanding how to add, remove, and manage subviews in your app's interface
Interface Builder vs code-based design — learning when to use each approach for building interfaces
UIAppearance protocol — customizing the appearance of your app's interface using this powerful protocol
TraitCollection class — working with this class to apply styles and layouts to multiple views at once

knowledge.what_it_is: A toolbar in iOS is a horizontal or vertical bar that displays a collection of controls, such as buttons, sliders, and labels, used to interact with an app. It is typically placed at the top or bottom of the screen.
what_you_will_learn: UIBarButtonItem — a basic toolbar item that can be customized with images, titles, and styles
UIToolbar — a container view that displays a collection of toolbar items; can be used to create custom toolbars with various layouts and styles
Toolbar Items — adding, removing, and managing individual toolbar items using the UIBarButtonItem class
Customizing Toolbar Appearance — changing font sizes, colors, and other visual properties of toolbar items
Toolbar Item Actions — handling user interactions with toolbar items, such as tapping or swiping
Storyboarding Toolbars — designing and configuring toolbars in Interface Builder (IB)
Toolbar Constraints — using Auto Layout to position and size toolbars within the app's view hierarchy

knowledge.what_it_is: A navigator is a type of view controller that manages a stack of child view controllers, providing a way to navigate through an app's UI hierarchy.
what_you_will_learn: Navigation Controller — a built-in iOS class that manages a stack of view controllers and provides navigation between them
UINavigationControllerDelegate — a protocol for customizing the navigation controller's behavior, such as handling push/pop transitions
Push Segue — a type of segue that adds a new view controller to the top of the navigation stack
Present Modally — presenting a view controller modally, either full-screen or in a popover
Tab Bar Controller — a container view controller that manages multiple child view controllers and provides tab bar navigation
UITabBarControllerDelegate — a protocol for customizing the tab bar controller's behavior, such as handling tab selection
Navigation Stack — the ordered list of view controllers managed by a navigation controller
UINavigationControllerTransitionStyle — an enum specifying the animation style used when pushing or popping view controllers

knowledge.what_it_is: Xcode's editors are where you design, code, and debug your iOS app. The editor supports multiple languages including Swift and Objective-C.
what_you_will_learn: Text Editor — understanding the basic text editing features in Xcode, such as syntax highlighting, auto-completion, and code folding
Assistant Editor — learning how to use the assistant editor for previewing your app's UI, inspecting properties, and debugging issues
Navigator Editor — mastering the navigator editor for navigating through your project's files, folders, and dependencies
Debugging in Xcode — using the debugger to step through code, set breakpoints, and analyze memory usage
Code Completion — understanding how to use Xcode's code completion features, including auto-suggestions, code snippets, and refactoring tools
Version Control Integration — learning how to integrate version control systems like Git into your workflow within Xcode
Editor Settings — customizing the editor settings for your preferred coding style, such as indentation, line wrapping, and syntax coloring

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, Storyboards are a visual representation of an app's user interface, allowing developers to design and layout screens using drag-and-drop components. They are a key feature of Xcode and provide a way to create complex interfaces without writing code.
what_you_will_learn: Storyboard Segues — connecting view controllers in Storyboards to navigate between screens
Segue Types (Push, Present, Modal) — understanding the different types of segues and when to use them
Embed Segue — embedding a view controller within another view controller using a segue
Unwind Segue — navigating back to a previous screen using an unwind segue
Storyboard Ids — assigning unique identifiers to view controllers in Storyboards for referencing
Constraints (Auto Layout) — setting up constraints in Storyboards to position and size UI elements
Size Classes — designing adaptive layouts using Size Classes in Storyboards
Trait Collections — creating custom trait collections to apply styles and behaviors to views

knowledge.what_it_is: Breakpoints are a debugging feature in Xcode that allow you to pause execution of your iOS app at specific points, inspect variables and memory, and step through code.
what_you_will_learn: Setting breakpoints — creating and managing breakpoints in the Breakpoint Navigator
Conditional breakpoints — setting breakpoints to trigger only under certain conditions
Breakpoint actions — running custom code when a breakpoint is hit, such as logging or taking a screenshot
Stepping through code — navigating through your app's execution using the Step Over, Step Into, and Step Out buttons
Inspecting variables and memory — viewing and manipulating the values of variables and memory locations at breakpoints
Using the Debug Navigator — accessing and managing breakpoints, threads, and other debugging information in one place
Enabling and disabling breakpoints — temporarily or permanently removing breakpoints from your app's execution
Breakpoint types — understanding the different types of breakpoints available, including exception, function, and data breakpoints

knowledge.what_it_is: The Debug Navigator is an Xcode feature that allows developers to inspect and debug their iOS app's execution flow, including threads, stack frames, and breakpoints.
what_you_will_learn: Breakpoints — setting and managing breakpoints in the Debug Navigator, including conditional breakpoints and breakpoint actions
Threads — understanding and debugging multi-threaded apps using the Threads tab in the Debug Navigator
Stack Frames — inspecting and navigating stack frames to identify issues with function calls and returns
Variables View — accessing and inspecting variables in scope at any point during execution, including local and global variables
Expression Evaluation — evaluating arbitrary expressions in the Variables View to debug complex logic
Call Stacks — understanding and debugging call stacks to identify recursive function calls or infinite loops

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, groups are a type of container view that can hold other views and provide a way to organize and layout content on the screen.
what_you_will_learn: Grouped Views — creating containers for views using the Group class in Xcode
Auto Layout Constraints — adding constraints to group views to define their size, position, and relationship with other views
Stack Views vs Groups — understanding when to use each type of view container for layout and organization
Adding Subviews to Groups — adding child views to a group using the addSubview method or Interface Builder
Group Properties — setting properties like background color, corner radius, and shadow on groups in code or IB
Group Constraints — creating constraints between groups and other views to define their layout and spacing

knowledge.what_it_is: In Xcode, project files are the foundation of an iOS app's structure and organization. They contain metadata, settings, and configurations that define how the app will be built and deployed.
what_you_will_learn: Workspace — the top-level container for managing multiple projects and configurations
Project Navigator — a hierarchical view of project files, including targets, schemes, and assets
Targets — individual units of work within a project, defining build settings and dependencies
Schemes — configurations that define how to build and run a target, including debugging and testing options
Build Settings — customizable properties for each target, such as compiler flags and optimization levels
Info.plist — a critical file containing metadata about the app, including its name, description, and icons
Asset Catalogs — collections of images, fonts, and other resources used by the app
Resource Files — files that are embedded in the app's bundle, such as storyboards and xib files

knowledge.what_it_is: Stepping refers to the process of navigating through a series of views or screens in an iOS app, often used in table view controllers, collection view controllers, and other data-driven UI components. It is essential for creating user-friendly and intuitive interfaces.
what_you_will_learn: Table View Cells — customizing cell appearance, layout, and behavior using protocols and delegates
Cell Reuse — optimizing performance by reusing table view cells with different configurations
Section Headers and Footers — adding custom views to section headers and footers for improved user experience
Indexing and Section Titles — implementing indexing and displaying section titles in table views
Collection View Layouts — using layout attributes, flow layouts, and grid layouts to arrange collection view cells
Cell Selection and Deselection — handling cell selection and deselection events, including multiple selection modes
Stepping Animations — creating custom animations for stepping transitions between views or screens

knowledge.what_it_is: Interface Builder (IB) is a visual design tool in Xcode, used to create and layout user interfaces for iOS apps. It allows designers and developers to work together on UI designs without writing code.
what_you_will_learn: Storyboard — creating and managing the main storyboard file (.storyboard), which contains the app's UI hierarchy
Scene — a single view in the storyboard, representing a screen or view controller
View Controller — a class that manages a scene's UI and behavior, often created programmatically or using IB
Auto Layout — setting up constraints to position and size views within their superviews
Constraints — defining relationships between views, such as leading/trailing edges, top/bottom edges, and width/height
Size Classes — designing for different screen sizes and orientations using Xcode's built-in size classes feature
IBOutlets — connecting UI elements to code in a view controller, allowing for dynamic updates and interactions
IBActions — linking UI events (e.g., button taps) to code in a view controller, enabling user interactions

knowledge.what_it_is: IBOutlets are properties in an iOS view controller that connect to user interface elements (UI controls) created in Interface Builder, allowing you to access and manipulate them programmatically.
what_you_will_learn: Outlet collection — a single outlet that connects to multiple UI controls, reducing code duplication
IBOutlet property declaration — declaring outlets in your view controller's header file using the @property directive
Storyboard Segue — connecting an IBAction to a segue in Interface Builder, allowing you to navigate between view controllers
Outlet connection issues — common problems and solutions when connecting outlets to UI controls, such as missing or duplicate connections
IBOutlet properties vs. local variables — understanding the differences and best practices for using outlets versus local variables
Using IBOutlets with custom classes — how to create and use custom classes that have IBOutlets
Outlet caching — optimizing outlet performance by caching outlet values in memory

knowledge.what_it_is: IBActions are a way to connect user interface elements in Interface Builder (IB) to code, allowing you to perform actions when users interact with your app's UI.
what_you_will_learn: Connecting IBOutlets and IBActions — linking UI elements to code using the Assistant Editor
Action Segues — creating segues between view controllers that trigger specific actions or transitions
Outlet Collections — grouping multiple UI elements together for easier management in code
Control Events — responding to user interactions such as taps, gestures, and keyboard input
Target-Action Pattern — using IBActions to send messages to specific objects or methods when a UI element is interacted with

knowledge.what_it_is: User interactions are how users engage with an iOS app, including tapping, swiping, pinching, and other gestures.
what_you_will_learn: Touch events — handling user touches, taps, and gestures using UIGestureRecognizer subclasses (e.g., UITapGestureRecognizer, UISwipeGestureRecognizer)
Gesture recognizers — creating custom gesture recognizers to handle complex interactions (e.g., pinch-to-zoom, drag-and-drop)
Long press — implementing long press actions for context menus or other UI elements
Drag-and-drop — enabling users to drag and drop items between views using UIDragInteraction and UIDropInteraction
Swipe gestures — handling swipe gestures for navigation or other interactions (e.g., UISwipeGestureRecognizer, UISwipeActionsConfiguration)
3D Touch — supporting 3D Touch on iPhone devices with force touch gestures (e.g., peek-and-pop, quick actions)
Accessibility — implementing accessibility features to enable users with disabilities to interact with the app (e.g., VoiceOver, AssistiveTouch)
Haptic feedback — providing haptic feedback for user interactions using UINotificationFeedbackGenerator and UIImpactFeedbackGenerator

knowledge.what_it_is: XIBs (XML Interface Builder files) are a way to design and create user interfaces for iOS apps using Xcode's visual interface builder. They allow developers to drag-and-drop UI components, set properties, and connect outlets without writing code.
what_you_will_learn: Storyboard vs XIB: Understanding the differences between storyboards and XIBs in iOS development
XIB file structure — understanding the XML elements that make up a XIB file, including views, controls, and constraints
View Controllers in XIBs — how to create and configure view controllers within a XIB file
Auto Layout in XIBs — using Auto Layout constraints to position and size UI components within a XIB file
IBOutlet connections — connecting outlets to UI components in a XIB file, including labels, buttons, and text fields
IBActions — creating IBAction methods for responding to user interactions with UI components in a XIB file
View hierarchy in XIBs — understanding how views are organized within a XIB file, including superviews and subviews

knowledge.what_it_is: State management in iOS refers to the process of managing and updating the application's state, which includes user interface elements, data, and other relevant information. This is crucial for maintaining a responsive and efficient user experience.
what_you_will_learn: UserDefaults — storing small amounts of data persistently using Apple's built-in framework
Property List (plist) files — serializing and deserializing data to/from plist files for storage and retrieval
Core Data — using Apple's Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) framework for complex data modeling and management
Realm Database — a popular, lightweight alternative to Core Data for storing and managing data in iOS apps
UserDefaults with Codable — encoding and decoding custom objects using the Codable protocol for efficient storage and retrieval
MVVM Architecture Pattern — separating application logic into Model-View-ViewModel layers for better state management and decoupling
Combine Framework — using Apple's reactive programming framework to manage and update state in a declarative way

knowledge.what_it_is: Navigation is a crucial aspect of iOS app development, enabling users to move between different screens and views within an app. It involves managing the app's navigation hierarchy, including pushing and popping view controllers, presenting and dismissing modals, and handling segues.
what_you_will_learn: View Controllers — managing the app's navigation hierarchy, including pushing and popping view controllers
Navigation Controllers — a container view controller that manages a stack of child view controllers, enabling push and pop operations
Segues — a way to transition between view controllers, including manual segues and segue identifiers
Presenting and Dismissing Views — presenting modals, alerts, and action sheets, as well as dismissing them
Navigation Stack — managing the stack of view controllers, including pushing, popping, and accessing the topmost view controller
UINavigationControllerDelegate — a protocol that allows you to customize navigation controller behavior, such as handling segue transitions
UIPageViewController — a view controller that enables swipe-based navigation between multiple child view controllers
Tab Bar Controllers — managing tab bar items and their corresponding view controllers

knowledge.what_it_is: Data binding is a mechanism in iOS that connects user interface elements to data sources, enabling automatic updates when data changes.
what_you_will_learn: IBOutlet — declaring outlets in Interface Builder or code, linking UI components to properties
IBInspectable and IBDesignable — customizing views and controls using Xcode's built-in inspectors and designables
Data Binding with Bindings (KVO) — observing property changes and updating UI elements automatically
Core Data — a powerful framework for managing model data, including entities, relationships, and fetch requests
ViewModel pattern — separating business logic from the view controller using a mediator object
MVVM architecture — using a ViewModel to bind data to the view, decoupling presentation logic from the view controller
RxSwift or ReactiveKit — reactive programming frameworks for handling asynchronous data streams and binding UI elements
Combine framework — Apple's modern reactive programming framework for building asynchronous pipelines

knowledge.what_it_is: View transitions are animations that occur when switching between views in an iOS app, used to create a seamless user experience.
what_you_will_learn: Push and Pop Segues — animating view changes using Storyboard segues
Presenting and Dismissing Views — understanding how to present and dismiss modally presented views with animations
Custom View Transitions — creating custom transition animations using UIViewPropertyAnimator, CATransition, or other animation libraries
Transition Styles — applying different transition styles such as crossfade, page curl, or flip
View Controller Containment — managing view controller containment hierarchies for smooth transitions between views
Modal Presentation Styles — understanding the different modal presentation styles and how to animate them
UINavigationController Transitions — animating view changes when using a UINavigationController
CATransition — using Core Animation's CATransition class to create custom transition animations

knowledge.what_it_is: Navigation controllers and segues are used to manage navigation between view controllers in an iOS app, providing a way to present new views, dismiss current views, and handle complex navigation flows.
what_you_will_learn: UINavigationController — the default navigation controller provided by iOS, managing a stack of view controllers and handling common navigation tasks
Segue — a transition between two view controllers, created in Storyboard or programmatically using UIStoryboardSegue
Push Segue — pushing a new view controller onto the navigation stack, replacing the current topmost view controller
Present Modally — presenting a new view controller modally, covering the entire screen and allowing user interaction with the background view controller
Dismiss View Controller — dismissing the current presented or pushed view controller, returning control to the previous view controller in the navigation stack
Navigation Stack — the ordered list of view controllers managed by a UINavigationController, used for navigation and back button behavior
Segue Identifier — a unique string identifier assigned to each segue in Storyboard, used to programmatically identify and perform segues
Storyboard Segue — creating and configuring segues visually in Storyboard, including setting segue identifiers and attributes

knowledge.what_it_is: Pushing and presenting views are fundamental concepts in iOS development, allowing users to interact with multiple screens and navigate through your app.
what_you_will_learn: Presenting a view controller — displaying a new screen on top of the current one using presentViewController:animated:completion:
Modally presenting a view controller — presenting a view controller modally using presentModalViewController:animated: or present(_:animated:completion:)
Pushing a view controller onto a navigation stack — navigating through multiple screens using pushViewController:animated:
Presenting a view controller with a custom transition — animating the presentation of a view controller using presentViewController:animated:transition:completion:
Using segues to present views — defining segues in Storyboard and presenting views automatically
Dismissing a presented view controller — removing a presented view from the screen using dismissViewControllerAnimated:completion:
Handling view controller dismissal events — implementing viewWillDisappear:, viewDidDisappear:, or viewWillTransition(to:) to handle view dismissal
Using unwind segues to navigate back — defining an unwind segue in Storyboard and navigating back automatically

knowledge.what_it_is: View presentation and dismissal are essential iOS concepts, allowing developers to manage the visibility of views within an app's UI hierarchy.
what_you_will_learn: Modal View Controllers — presenting view controllers modally with presentViewController:animated:completion:, dismissing with dismissViewControllerAnimated:completion:
Segue-based Navigation — using segues to navigate between view controllers and present/dismiss them
Push Segue — pushing a new view controller onto the navigation stack, creating a new view controller instance
Present Modally — presenting a view controller modally with presentViewController:animated:completion:, specifying presentation style (e.g., full screen)
Dismissal Animations — customizing dismissal animations using animator objects and completion handlers
Transitioning Delegate — implementing the UIViewControllerTransitioningDelegate protocol to customize transitions between view controllers
View Controller Containment — using a container view controller to present child view controllers, managing their lifecycle and presentation
Navigation Stack Management — pushing, popping, and presenting view controllers on the navigation stack

knowledge.what_it_is: Auto Layout is a system of constraints that automatically adjusts the size and position of user interface elements on an iOS device, ensuring proper alignment and spacing regardless of screen size or orientation.
what_you_will_learn: Constraints — defining relationships between views using anchors (leading/trailing/top/bottom) and edges
Layout Guides — creating invisible guides to help align views with the safe area and other elements
Safe Area — understanding how Auto Layout respects the device's screen boundaries, including notch, home indicator, and rounded corners
Stack Views — using a container view to simplify layout by stacking child views horizontally or vertically
NSLayoutConstraint — creating and managing constraints programmatically using this class
UILayoutGuide — creating custom guides for complex layouts that require precise control over spacing and alignment

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, Views and View Controllers are fundamental building blocks for creating user interfaces. A View represents a rectangular area on the screen that displays content, while a View Controller manages a View's lifecycle and provides functionality.
what_you_will_learn: UIView — the base class for all views in iOS, providing basic properties like frame, bounds, and layer
UIViewController — the base class for view controllers, responsible for managing a view's lifecycle and presenting other views
View Hierarchy — understanding how views are organized in a tree-like structure, with parent-child relationships between views
Auto Layout — using constraints to position and size views relative to their superviews or other views
Storyboard Segues — defining transitions between views using storyboards, including modal, push, and present segues
Segue Types — understanding the different types of segues available in iOS, such as show (modal), show detail (push), and present
View Controller Lifecycle Methods — learning about methods like viewDidLoad(), viewDidAppear(), and didReceiveMemoryWarning() that are called at various points during a view controller's lifecycle
Presenting and Dismissing Views — understanding how to present and dismiss views using presentViewController:animated:completion: and dismissViewControllerAnimated:completion:
Navigation Controllers — using UINavigationControllers to manage a stack of view controllers and provide navigation between them

knowledge.what_it_is: A navigation stack is a data structure used to manage the presentation of views in an iOS app, allowing users to navigate through multiple screens while maintaining a history of previously visited views.
what_you_will_learn: UINavigationController — a built-in iOS class that manages a stack-based navigation system, providing methods for pushing and popping view controllers
pushViewController:animated: — method used to push a new view controller onto the navigation stack, animating the transition if desired
popViewControllerAnimated: — method used to remove the top-most view controller from the navigation stack, animating the transition if desired
presentViewController:animated:completion: — method used to present a view controller modally, adding it to the navigation stack if desired
dismissViewControllerAnimated:completion: — method used to dismiss a presented view controller, removing it from the navigation stack if desired
Navigation Controller's View Controllers Array — an array that stores all view controllers in the navigation stack, allowing for manual manipulation of the stack
UINavigationBar — a built-in iOS class that provides a customizable navigation bar at the top of each screen in the navigation stack
UIBarButtonItem — a built-in iOS class that represents a button or other interactive element in the navigation bar

knowledge.what_it_is: NavigationView is a high-level SwiftUI view that provides a navigation stack and automatic support for back buttons, tabs, and other common navigation patterns.
what_you_will_learn: NavigationView — a high-level SwiftUI view that encapsulates a navigation stack
NavigationLink — a view that creates a link to another destination in the app's navigation stack
DestinationLink — a specialized NavigationLink for creating links to external destinations
NavigationStack — a new, more powerful alternative to NavigationView (iOS 16+)
TabView — a SwiftUI view that presents multiple tabs of content within a single screen
Sheet — a modal presentation style that slides up from the bottom of the screen
FullScreenCover — a full-screen modal presentation style that covers the entire screen
NavigationPath — a type that represents a sequence of destinations in a navigation stack

knowledge.what_it_is: Modals and navigation are essential components of iOS app design, allowing users to interact with your app in a flexible and intuitive way. Modals provide a separate view that can be presented on top of the main app screen, while navigation enables users to move between different views within the app.
what_you_will_learn: ModalPresentationStyle — configuring modal presentation styles (e.g., fullScreen, pageSheet) for customizing the appearance and behavior of modals
UINavigationController — using a navigation controller to manage view hierarchy and provide back button functionality
NavigationLink — creating links between views in SwiftUI, enabling seamless navigation between screens
@StateObject vs @ObservedObject — understanding when to use state objects or observed objects for managing view data and dependencies
Coordinator pattern — implementing the coordinator pattern to manage complex app flows and modal presentations
SwiftUI NavigationView — using a navigation view in SwiftUI to provide a customizable navigation bar and handle navigation between views
Modal transition animations — creating custom animations for presenting modals, such as slide-in or fade-in effects

knowledge.what_it_is: NavigationLink is a SwiftUI view that enables navigation between views in an iOS app, providing a declarative way to define routes and handle navigation.
what_you_will_learn: Creating NavigationLinks with destination — specifying the view to navigate to using the @ViewBuilder or @SceneBuilder
Passing parameters with navigationLink(destination:) — passing data from one view to another through URL encoding
Handling link activation with .link — defining a custom action when a NavigationLink is activated, such as presenting a modal or pushing a new view onto the navigation stack
Using navigationLink(isActive:) — conditionally displaying a NavigationLink based on the current state of the app
Implementing custom navigation links with Link — creating a custom link that can be used to navigate between views in an iOS app

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, building interfaces refers to creating user-facing screens and views using SwiftUI or UIKit frameworks. This involves designing and implementing layouts, user interactions, and visual elements that provide a seamless experience for users.
what_you_will_learn: Views — the basic building blocks of UI in SwiftUI, used to create custom layouts and designs
Stack Views — a type of view that arranges its children in a vertical or horizontal stack, useful for creating complex layouts
HStack and VStack — horizontal and vertical stacks of views, respectively, used to create flexible layouts
Grids — a layout system in SwiftUI that allows for easy creation of grid-based interfaces
Safe Area — understanding how to use safe areas to ensure your app's UI is properly constrained on different devices
Constraints — learning how to use Auto Layout constraints to position and size views correctly
Layout Guides — using layout guides to create custom spacing and alignment in your app's UI

knowledge.what_it_is: In SwiftUI, Views are the building blocks of user interfaces, responsible for rendering visual content. Modifiers are used to customize View behavior, layout, and appearance.
what_you_will_learn: View — a self-contained piece of UI that can be composed with other views
Modifier — a function that takes a view as input and returns a modified version of it
VStack, HStack, ZStack — stack layouts for arranging views horizontally or vertically
Spacer — a view that takes up available space in its parent layout
Frame, Rectangle, Circle — shapes used to draw custom graphics
Color, Text, Image — basic views for displaying text and images
Padding, Border, Shadow — modifiers for adding visual effects to views

knowledge.what_it_is: SwiftUI is Apple's UI framework for building iOS, iPadOS, watchOS, and tvOS apps, introduced in 2019 as a replacement for UIKit. It provides declarative syntax, automatic layout management, and built-in support for gestures, animations, and other features.
what_you_will_learn: Views — the fundamental building blocks of SwiftUI apps, replacing traditional UIViews
Modifiers — functions that modify views at runtime, such as .padding(), .border(), and .cornerRadius()
Layouts — automatic layout management using StackView, HStack, VStack, and other layout types
State and Bindings — managing app state with @State, @Binding, and @ObservedObject
Data Flow — understanding how SwiftUI apps update their views in response to user input and data changes
Animations and Transitions — creating smooth animations using Animation, Transition, and other APIs
Gestures — handling user interactions with TapGesture, DragGesture, LongPressGesture, and other gesture types
Navigation — building navigation stacks using NavigationView, NavigationLink, and other APIs
Modals and Sheets — presenting modals and sheets using .sheet() and .fullScreenCover()
Alerts and ActionSheets — displaying alerts and action sheets using Alert and ActionSheet
Environment and EnvironmentValues — accessing environment values and managing app-wide settings
Dependency Injection — injecting dependencies into views using @StateObject, @ObservedObject, and other APIs
Async/Await and Tasks — writing asynchronous code with async/await and Task
JSON / XML Parsing — parsing JSON and XML data in SwiftUI apps using URLSession and other APIs

knowledge.what_it_is: Human Interface Guidelines (HIG) are Apple's design standards and guidelines for creating user-friendly, intuitive, and visually appealing iOS apps. They provide a set of principles, patterns, and best practices to ensure consistency across the App Store.
what_you_will_learn: Visual Hierarchy — structuring content using size, color, position, and typography to guide the user's attention
Typography — selecting fonts, font sizes, and line heights to create a clear and readable text hierarchy
Color — choosing colors that are accessible, consistent with Apple's brand guidelines, and meet WCAG 2.1 standards
Iconography — designing and using icons consistently throughout the app to represent actions, objects, or concepts
Button Design — creating buttons that are visually distinct from other UI elements, provide clear labels, and follow accessibility guidelines
Alerts and Modals — presenting alerts and modals in a way that is clear, concise, and easy to understand
Table Views and Collection Views — using these views to display data in a structured and organized manner
Navigation — designing navigation patterns that are consistent with Apple's guidelines and provide an intuitive user experience

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Animation is a powerful framework in iOS that allows developers to create complex animations and interactions with ease. It provides a high-level, declarative API for animating views, layers, and other visual elements.
what_you_will_learn: Layer hierarchy — understanding the hierarchical structure of CALayers and how they interact
Animations basics — creating simple animations using CABasicAnimation and CAKeyframeAnimation
Keyframe animation — animating properties over time with precise control
Spring and damping — fine-tuning animation timing and behavior
Timing functions — customizing animation speed and acceleration curves
Animation groups — combining multiple animations into a single, coordinated sequence
CATransaction — managing animation context and ensuring proper completion
CAAction — creating custom animation actions for complex behaviors

knowledge.what_it_is: Lottie is an open-source animation library developed by Airbnb, which allows iOS developers to animate vector graphics and create complex animations using a simple JSON data model. It is widely used in mobile apps and has become a standard for animating UI components.
what_you_will_learn: Importing Lottie animations into Xcode projects — adding .json files to the project, importing them into Storyboards or SwiftUI views
Using Lottie animations with UIKit — configuring animation properties (speed, loop count), handling animation events (start, stop, pause)
Creating custom Lottie animations from scratch using Adobe After Effects and exporting as JSON files
Optimizing Lottie animations for performance — reducing file size, minimizing memory usage, using caching mechanisms
Integrating Lottie with SwiftUI — using the `LottieView` component, configuring animation properties (speed, loop count), handling animation events (start, stop, pause)
Using Lottie animations in conjunction with other iOS frameworks and libraries (e.g. Core Animation, UIKit Dynamics)
Customizing Lottie animations using JSON data model — modifying animation properties (color, size, position), adding custom animations
Implementing error handling for Lottie animations — catching and displaying errors when loading or playing animations

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Animation is Apple's powerful, high-performance graphics and animation framework for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS. It provides a unified way to create animations, transitions, and other visual effects across multiple platforms.
what_you_will_learn: Layers — creating and managing layers in the layer tree, including CALayer and CAReplicatorLayer
Animations — creating keyframe animations, spring animations, and implicit animations using CABasicAnimation, CAKeyframeAnimation, and CAMediaTimingFunction
Transitions — animating view changes with CATransition, including fade, move, push, reveal, and slide transitions
Actions — performing actions on layers, such as rotation, scaling, and translation, using CAAction and CAAnimationGroup
Constraints — defining layout constraints for animations using NSLayoutConstraint and CAConstraint
Keyframe Animations — creating complex animations with keyframes, including easing functions and animation curves
Implicit Animations — animating views without explicit animation code using Auto Layout and the built-in animation system
CATransform3D — working with 3D transformations, including rotations, scaling, and translations

knowledge.what_it_is: MVC (Model-View-Controller) is a design pattern used in iOS development to separate an application's logic into three interconnected components: Model, View, and Controller. It is a fundamental concept in iOS programming and is widely used for building complex user interfaces.
what_you_will_learn: Models — data structures that represent the application's data, using Swift structs or classes
Views — responsible for rendering the user interface, using UIKit or SwiftUI
Controllers — manage the flow of data between Models and Views, handling user interactions and updating the UI
Data Binding — linking Model properties to View elements, enabling automatic updates when data changes
Delegate Pattern — implementing delegate protocols in Controllers to notify other components of events or state changes
Notification Center — using NSNotificationCenter to broadcast notifications between Components
Storyboard Segues — defining transitions between View Controllers using Storyboards
Segue Identifier — assigning a unique identifier to each segue for easy identification and management

knowledge.what_it_is: Model-View-Presenter (MVP) is an architectural pattern for iOS apps, separating concerns into three interconnected components: Model, View, and Presenter. It's a variation of the MVC pattern, with the Presenter acting as an intermediary between the View and Model.
what_you_will_learn: Presenter — the central component that coordinates data flow between View and Model, handling business logic and presentation
View — responsible for rendering user interface elements, receiving input from the user, and updating the Presenter with changes
Model — encapsulates app's data and business logic, providing a layer of abstraction between the Presenter and data storage
Data Binding — binding View to Model using KVO (Key-Value Observing) or reactive frameworks like RxSwift
Presenter-View Interaction — handling user input, updating the View with new data, and notifying the Presenter of changes
Presenter-Model Interaction — fetching and storing data in the Model, updating the Presenter with new information

knowledge.what_it_is: Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) is a software architectural pattern used in iOS development to separate application logic into three interconnected components: Model, View, and ViewModel. The ViewModel acts as an intermediary between the View and the Model, exposing the data and functionality of the Model in a form that can be easily consumed by the View.
what_you_will_learn: ViewModel — a class that exposes the data and functionality of the Model to the View, providing a layer of abstraction and decoupling
Data Binding — automatically updating the View when the underlying data changes, using frameworks like Combine or RxSwift
Command Pattern — encapsulating actions and commands in the ViewModel, allowing for easy separation of concerns and unit testing
Dependency Injection — injecting dependencies into the ViewModel, making it easier to test and maintain
Data Model — defining the structure and behavior of the data used by the application, using frameworks like Realm or Core Data
View State Management — managing the state of the View, including animations and transitions, using libraries like SwiftUI or UIKit
ViewModel Lifecycle — understanding how the ViewModel is created and destroyed, and how to manage its lifecycle
Error Handling — handling errors and exceptions in the ViewModel, providing a robust and reliable user experience

knowledge.what_it_is: MVVM-C (Model-View-ViewModel with Coordinator) is an architectural pattern for iOS apps, combining the benefits of MVVM and a centralized coordinator to manage complex app flows. It's designed to handle multiple view controllers, navigation, and data management efficiently.
what_you_will_learn: ViewModels — managing business logic, exposing data to views through properties and commands
Coordinators — centralizing app flow logic, handling navigation, and managing dependencies between view controllers
Dependency Injection (DI) — decoupling components using a container or framework like Swinject or Dagger
RxSwift or Combine — using reactive programming libraries for data binding and event handling in ViewModels
Data Binding — synchronizing data between ViewModels and Views using property observers, bindings, or reactive streams
MVVM-C Architecture Layers — understanding the separation of concerns between Coordinators, ViewModels, and Views
ViewModel Lifetime Management — managing ViewModel instances, including creation, destruction, and caching
App Flow Navigation — handling complex app flows using a Coordinator, including push/pop navigation and tab bar management

knowledge.what_it_is: VIPER is an architectural pattern for iOS apps, developed by Gabriele Petronella and later maintained by the community. It stands for View-Interactor-Presenter-Entity-Router, and is designed to separate concerns and make code more maintainable.
what_you_will_learn: View — responsible for rendering UI components, using UIKit or SwiftUI
Interactor — handles business logic, interacts with Presenter and Entity
Presenter — acts as an intermediary between Interactor and View, handling data transformation and validation
Entity — represents the app's domain model, encapsulating data and behavior
Router — manages navigation between screens, using protocols like UINavigationController or UINavigationRouter
Dependency Injection — injecting dependencies into components, promoting loose coupling and testability
Clean Architecture — applying VIPER to create a layered architecture, separating presentation from business logic

knowledge.what_it_is: TCA (The Composability Architecture) is a reactive, declarative architecture pattern developed by Apple for building iOS and macOS apps. It provides a unified way to manage state, side effects, and interactions between view controllers, views, and other UI components.
what_you_will_learn: Value Types — immutable data structures that can be used as properties of view models
Environment — a container for injecting dependencies into view models, similar to dependency injection in other frameworks
Feature Flags — a way to toggle features on or off at runtime without recompiling the app
Effects — a way to handle side effects such as network requests, database operations, and file I/O in a reactive manner
Interactions — a way to manage interactions between view controllers, views, and other UI components, including animations and gestures
Store — a container for storing and managing application state, similar to Redux or MobX
ViewModels — a way to separate presentation logic from business logic in iOS apps
Combine Publishers — a way to handle asynchronous data streams and events in a reactive manner

knowledge.what_it_is: RxSwift is a popular reactive programming library for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS apps, providing a simple and efficient way to handle asynchronous data streams and events. It's widely used in production by top companies like Apple, Google, and Facebook.
what_you_will_learn: Observables — creating and managing sequences of values using the Observable class
Operators — combining and transforming observables with methods like map, filter, reduce, and switchMap
Subjects — sharing data between operators and observers using Subjects (BehaviorSubject, ReplaySubject, PublishSubject)
Schedulers — executing tasks on specific threads or queues using Schedulers (SerialDispatchQueueScheduler, ConcurrentDispatchQueueScheduler)
Subscribers — handling events and errors in observables with Subscriber protocol and onNext, onError, onComplete methods
DisposeBag — managing subscriptions and disposing of resources when no longer needed
RxSwift with MVVM — integrating RxSwift with Model-View-ViewModel architecture for data binding and state management

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, Observables and Observers are key concepts in Reactive Programming that enable asynchronous data processing and event handling.
what_you_will_learn: RxSwift — a popular library for reactive programming on iOS, providing Observable sequences for handling asynchronous data
Observable — a sequence of events or values that can be subscribed to by Observers, used for handling asynchronous data in RxSwift
Observer — an object that subscribes to an Observable and receives notifications when new values are emitted, used for handling asynchronous data in RxSwift
subscribe() — method on Observable that allows an Observer to receive notifications when new values are emitted
unsubscribe() — method on Subscription that stops receiving notifications from the Observable
Disposable — a class that manages the lifetime of a subscription and ensures it is properly cleaned up when no longer needed
