Subject — a special type of Observable that can be used as both an Observer and an Observable, allowing for bidirectional communication between objects
PublishSubject — a specific type of Subject that allows multiple Observers to receive notifications from the same sequence of events

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS development, subjects refer to the publishers of values that can be subscribed to by observers, enabling reactive programming and asynchronous data handling.
what_you_will_learn: Publishers — creating and managing subject instances for publishing values
Subjects vs. Observables — understanding the differences between these two fundamental concepts in RxSwift
PublishSubject — a type of subject that buffers all incoming events until subscribed to
BehaviorSubject — a type of subject that caches the last value emitted until subscribed to
ReplaySubject — a type of subject that buffers and replays all incoming events to new subscribers
Variable — a wrapper around BehaviorSubject for easier usage in iOS apps
Driver — a wrapper around PublishSubject for easier usage with RxSwift in iOS apps

knowledge.what_it_is: Operators are a crucial part of RxSwift, allowing you to transform and manipulate data streams in reactive programming. They enable you to handle errors, combine multiple observables, and perform complex operations on data.
what_you_will_learn: Map — transforming individual elements in an observable sequence using a provided function
Filter — selecting items from an observable sequence based on a specified condition
FlatMap — combining the elements of two or more sequences into one, while also handling errors and disconnection
Concat — merging multiple observables into one, preserving their original order
Merge — combining multiple observables into one, allowing for concurrent execution
SwitchToLatest — switching to the latest observable in a sequence when new ones are added
Take — selecting a specified number of elements from an observable sequence
RetryWhen — retrying failed operations based on a custom error handling strategy

knowledge.what_it_is: Scheduling is a mechanism to run tasks at specific times or intervals, allowing for efficient management of app resources and responsiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Dispatch Queue — a queue-based scheduling system that manages concurrent execution of tasks
Serial Dispatch Queue — a single-threaded dispatch queue that executes tasks sequentially
Concurrent Dispatch Queue — a multi-threaded dispatch queue that executes tasks concurrently
Operation Queue — a high-level scheduling system for managing asynchronous operations
GCD (Grand Central Dispatch) — a low-level API for creating and managing concurrent execution of tasks
Dispatch Group — a mechanism to group related tasks together for efficient execution
Dispatch Semaphore — a synchronization primitive that regulates access to shared resources
Timer — a scheduling system for running tasks at specific intervals or times

knowledge.what_it_is: Combine is Apple's modern, declarative framework for building reactive systems on iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS. It provides a more expressive and efficient way to handle asynchronous data flows compared to traditional callback-based or delegate-based approaches.
what_you_will_learn: Publishers — the core abstraction in Combine, representing streams of values that can be subscribed to
Subscribers — objects that observe publishers and react to changes in their output
Operators — functions that transform publisher outputs, such as map, filter, and reduce
Schedulers — a way to run code on specific threads or queues, ensuring proper concurrency and synchronization
Subjects — a type of publisher that can be used to share data between multiple subscribers
CurrentValueBehavior — a behavior that returns the current value of a subject when subscribed
Retained(while) — an operator that retains a subscriber until a specified condition is met or a certain time has passed
assign(to:) — an operator that assigns the output of a publisher to a property on a subscriber

knowledge.what_it_is: In iOS, publishers and subscribers are core concepts in reactive programming, where objects notify each other of changes to shared data without explicit coupling. Publishers produce values or events, while subscribers receive these notifications.
what_you_will_learn: Publishers — creating objects that emit values or events to be consumed by subscribers
Subscribers — objects that receive notifications from publishers and react accordingly
RxSwift Publishers — using Swift's built-in `Publisher` protocol for reactive programming, including `Just`, `Empty`, `Completed`, `Error`, and `Sequence` types
Combine Publishers — Apple's Combine framework provides a modern alternative to RxSwift, with its own set of publishers like `PassthroughSubject`, `CurrentValueSubject`, and `AnyPublisher`
Operator chaining — combining multiple publishers using operators like `map`, `filter`, and `reduce` to transform or aggregate output values
Error handling — understanding how to handle errors in publisher-subscriber chains, including error types like `Publishers.Failure` and `Combine.Publisher.Failure`
Cancelling subscriptions — learning how to properly cancel subscriptions when no longer needed to prevent memory leaks and improve app performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Operators and pipelines are essential concepts in reactive programming, allowing you to transform and combine data streams in a declarative way.
what_you_will_learn: Map operator — transforming elements of an observable sequence using a provided function
Filter operator — removing unwanted elements from an observable sequence based on a predicate
FlatMap operator — flattening sequences produced by an observable sequence, useful for handling nested data structures
Reduce operator — accumulating values in an observable sequence into a single output value
Scan operator — applying a cumulative computation to the elements of an observable sequence
Take and TakeLast operators — extracting a specified number of elements from the beginning or end of an observable sequence
Skip and SkipWhile operators — skipping elements at the beginning of an observable sequence based on a predicate
DistinctUntilChanged operator — removing consecutive duplicates from an observable sequence
DebounceTime operator — delaying the emission of items in an observable sequence until a specified time has elapsed

knowledge.what_it_is: Combine is Apple's modern, declarative framework for asynchronous programming on iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS, designed to replace RxSwift and other reactive frameworks. It provides a more expressive and efficient way to handle asynchronous data flows, making it easier to write clean, maintainable code.
what_you_will_learn: Publishers — the core abstraction in Combine, representing streams of values that can be subscribed to
Subscribers — objects that subscribe to publishers to receive values and handle errors
Operators — functions that transform or combine publisher outputs, such as map, filter, and reduce
Sink — a type of subscriber that handles the final output of a pipeline, often used for UI updates
PassthroughSubject — a subject that allows multiple subscribers to receive the same values
CurrentValueSubject — a subject that stores its current value and notifies subscribers when it changes
CombineLatest — an operator that combines the latest values from multiple publishers into a single output
SwitchToLatest — an operator that switches to the latest publisher in a group of publishers

knowledge.what_it_is: RxSwift is a popular reactive programming library for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS developed by RxCommunity. It provides a declarative way to handle asynchronous data streams, making it easier to write more concise and maintainable code.
what_you_will_learn: Observables — creating and manipulating data streams using operators like map, filter, and reduce
Subjects — sharing observables between publishers and subscribers, including BehaviorSubject, ReplaySubject, and PublishSubject
Operators — applying transformations to observables, such as flatMap, switchToLatest, and merge
Schedulers — executing tasks on different threads or queues using Schedulers.mainThread, .background, and .serialDispatchQueueScheduler
MVVM Architecture — separating presentation logic from business logic using a Model-View-ViewModel pattern with RxSwift
RxSwift Bindings — binding UI components to observables using control events like tap, swipe, and keyboard input
Error Handling — catching and propagating errors in RxSwift using try-catch blocks and error types like ErrorType
RxCocoa Integration — integrating RxSwift with Cocoa frameworks like UIKit and Core Data

knowledge.what_it_is: A delegate pattern in iOS allows an object to receive notifications and respond to events from another object, enabling loose coupling and encapsulation.
what_you_will_learn: Delegate protocol — defining a custom delegate protocol with required methods for handling events
Delegate instance creation — creating a delegate instance and setting it on the target object
Delegate method implementation — implementing required delegate methods to handle events
Notification handling — using notifications to notify the delegate of changes or events
Weak references — using weak references to avoid retain cycles between the delegate and target objects
Delegate chaining — creating a chain of delegates to allow multiple objects to respond to an event
Swift protocol extensions — using Swift protocol extensions to simplify delegate implementation

knowledge.what_it_is: A closure is a block of code that captures its own scope and can be executed outside of it, often used to implement callbacks and event handlers in iOS development.
what_you_will_learn: Capturing variables — Understanding how closures capture their surrounding scope's variables and properties
Lexical scoping — How closures use lexical scoping to access outer scope variables and functions
Closure types — Familiarizing yourself with the two main types of closures: block-based and function-based
Using blocks as callbacks — Learning how to pass closures as arguments to methods and functions
Capturing self — Understanding how to capture the current object's context using `self` or `weak self`
Avoiding retain cycles — Recognizing and preventing memory leaks caused by strong references between objects
Closures with ARC — How Automatic Reference Counting affects closures and their memory management

knowledge.what_it_is: A closure is a block of code that captures its own scope, allowing it to access variables from its surrounding environment even when executed elsewhere. In iOS development, closures are commonly used with blocks, which are similar but have some key differences.
what_you_will_learn: Block syntax — declaring and using blocks in iOS code, including the ^ symbol for capturing scope
Capturing values — understanding how to capture variables from the surrounding environment within a block or closure
Escaping blocks — learning when and how to use escaping blocks to capture self or other objects
Weak references — understanding how to use weak references to avoid retain cycles with captured objects
Closures vs. blocks — key differences between closures and blocks, including scoping and memory management
Using closures in iOS frameworks — applying closure concepts to real-world scenarios in UIKit, SwiftUI, and other frameworks

knowledge.what_it_is: Capturing values and memory management are essential concepts in iOS development, ensuring that your app runs efficiently and doesn't leak resources. This involves understanding how to capture values using closures, blocks, and other mechanisms, as well as managing memory through ARC (Automatic Reference Counting), MRC (Manual Reference Counting), and other techniques.
what_you_will_learn: Blocks — capturing values and executing code asynchronously, used for networking, UI updates, and more
Closures — retaining context and executing code when needed, used for delegate methods, completion handlers, and callbacks
ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) — memory management using smart pointers, reducing the risk of memory leaks and crashes
MRC (Manual Reference Counting) — explicit memory management using retain/release, necessary in certain situations like Core Data or third-party libraries
Weak References — avoiding retain cycles by using weak references to objects, ensuring they can be deallocated when no longer needed
Strong References — retaining ownership of objects and preventing them from being deallocated prematurely
Retain Cycles — understanding how to identify and break retain cycles that prevent objects from being deallocated
Memory Leaks — detecting and fixing memory leaks caused by retained objects or circular references

knowledge.what_it_is: Callback hell refers to the situation where multiple callbacks are nested within each other, making it difficult to read and maintain code. This occurs when asynchronous operations are performed using callbacks instead of more modern approaches like async/await or Combine.
what_you_will_learn: Nested callbacks — understanding how multiple callbacks can be chained together, leading to complex code structures
Callback pyramid — recognizing the signs of callback hell and how it can lead to code bloat and maintainability issues
Async operations with callbacks — learning how to perform asynchronous tasks using callbacks, including networking requests, database queries, and file I/O
Using dispatch_group for concurrent execution — understanding how to use DispatchGroup to execute multiple asynchronous operations concurrently
Avoiding callback hell with async/await — learning how to use async/await to write more readable and maintainable code
Combine framework basics — understanding the basics of Combine, including Publishers, Subscribers, and Operators
Using Combine to avoid callback hell — learning how to use Combine to simplify asynchronous operations and reduce callback nesting

knowledge.what_it_is: Core Data is Apple's high-level, object-orientated data management framework for iOS and macOS applications, providing a powerful way to manage model objects, relationships, and persistence.
what_you_will_learn: Managed Object Model (MOM) — creating a visual representation of your app's data structure using Xcode's Data Model Inspector
Entities — defining custom classes that represent individual records in the database, with attributes and relationships
Attributes — specifying properties for each entity, including data types, optional flags, and default values
Relationships — establishing connections between entities to model complex data structures
Fetch Requests — creating queries to retrieve specific data from the store using NSFetchRequest
Fetched Properties — defining computed attributes that are calculated on-the-fly based on stored data
Core Data Stack — understanding the architecture of a Core Data-based app, including the managed object context and persistent store coordinator

knowledge.what_it_is: User Defaults is a built-in iOS framework that allows you to store and retrieve small amounts of data, such as preferences or settings, on an app-by-app basis. It provides a simple way to persist data between app launches without requiring a database.
what_you_will_learn: NSUserDefaults — the primary class for working with User Defaults, providing methods for storing and retrieving data
Data Types — understanding how different types of data are stored in User Defaults, including numbers, strings, booleans, arrays, and dictionaries
Key-Value Pairs — learning how to store and retrieve data using key-value pairs, where each key is a string and each value is an object or primitive type
Synchronization — understanding how User Defaults synchronizes data across devices and app launches, including the use of iCloud and other cloud services
Data Persistence — learning how to persist data between app launches, even when the app is terminated or the device restarts
UserDefaults(suiteName:) — using this method to store and retrieve data for a specific suite name, allowing multiple apps to share data
UserDefaults.standard — accessing the shared instance of UserDefaults, which provides a convenient way to store and retrieve data
Data Validation — learning how to validate user input data before storing it in User Defaults

knowledge.what_it_is: The Keychain is a secure storage system provided by iOS, allowing developers to store sensitive data such as passwords, credit card numbers, and encryption keys. It uses end-to-end encryption and is accessible through the Keychain API.
what_you_will_learn: Keychain Access — accessing the Keychain from within Xcode using the built-in Keychain Access utility
SecItemAdd — adding a new item to the Keychain, including its type, attributes, and value
SecItemCopyMatching — retrieving an existing item from the Keychain based on its attributes or identifier
Keychain Item Attributes — understanding the different types of attributes that can be stored in the Keychain, such as kSecAttrAccessible and kSecAttrAccount
Access Control Lists (ACLs) — managing access to Keychain items using ACLs, including adding and removing users and groups
Keychain Item Types — familiarizing yourself with the different types of Keychain items, such as passwords, certificates, and encryption keys
Secure Coding Practices — implementing secure coding practices when working with the Keychain API, including handling errors and exceptions

knowledge.what_it_is: The iOS file system is a hierarchical organization of files and directories that store data on an iPhone or iPad, managed by the Foundation framework's URL and FileManager classes. It provides a way to read and write files, manage storage, and handle file-related tasks.
what_you_will_learn: URL — representing a file path using a string or NSURL object, used for reading and writing files
FileManager — managing files and directories, including creating, deleting, and copying items
Document Directory — storing user-generated content, such as documents, images, and videos
Cache Directory — storing temporary data, like cached images and resources
Temporary Directory — storing temporary files that are deleted when the app restarts
File Attributes — setting and getting metadata, including permissions, creation date, and modification date
File Access Control — managing access to files based on user identity and permissions
Data Protection — encrypting data stored in the file system using iOS's built-in encryption mechanisms

knowledge.what_it_is: SQLite is a self-contained, file-based relational database system used by iOS applications to store and manage structured data locally on the device.
what_you_will_learn: Core Data vs SQLite — understanding when to use each for data persistence in iOS apps
Database schema design — creating efficient table structures with primary keys, foreign keys, indexes, and constraints
SQL syntax basics — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements, subqueries, joins, aggregations, and indexing
SQLite database configuration — setting up a persistent store for an iOS app using NSPersistentContainer or FMDatabase
Data modeling with SQLite — representing complex data relationships between tables using foreign keys and many-to-many relationships
Query optimization techniques — improving performance by minimizing disk I/O, reducing query complexity, and leveraging indexes
Error handling and debugging — catching and logging SQLite errors, using NSPersistentContainer's built-in error handling, and debugging with SQLite logs

knowledge.what_it_is: Async/Await is a programming paradigm that allows developers to write asynchronous code that's easier to read and maintain, using the async/await syntax. It's widely used in iOS development for tasks like network requests, database queries, and file operations.
what_you_will_learn: async/await syntax — writing asynchronous code with a synchronous feel
DispatchQueue — executing tasks on a queue to avoid blocking the main thread
OperationQueue — managing concurrent operations with dependencies and priorities
URLSession — making network requests using a high-level API for HTTP, HTTPS, and more
AFNetworking (or Alamofire) — popular third-party networking library for iOS
PromiseKit — a promise-based API for handling asynchronous tasks in Swift
Combine — Apple's reactive framework for building asynchronous workflows
async/await with Combine — using async/await to write Combine pipelines
TaskGroup — executing multiple asynchronous tasks concurrently and waiting for all to complete

knowledge.what_it_is: Parsing in iOS refers to the process of extracting meaningful data from input streams, such as XML or JSON files, into a format that can be used by an iOS app. This is typically done using frameworks like NSXMLParser or NSJSONSerialization.
what_you_will_learn: NSXMLParser — parsing XML documents and handling events such as startElement, endElement, and characters
NSJSONSerialization — serializing and deserializing JSON data to and from Foundation objects like NSDictionary and NSArray
XML Parsing with NSXMLParserDelegate — implementing delegate methods to handle XML parsing events and errors
JSON Parsing with JSONObjectWithData — creating a JSON object from a data stream and accessing its properties
Swift Codable — using the Codable protocol to serialize and deserialize custom Swift types to and from JSON or XML
Property List (plist) files — working with plist files, which are used to store configuration data in iOS apps

knowledge.what_it_is: Serialization in iOS refers to converting objects into a format that can be written to storage or transmitted over a network, and vice versa. This process is crucial for data persistence, caching, and API communication.
what_you_will_learn: JSON serialization — using NSJSONSerialization to convert between JSON strings and native Objective-C/ Swift objects
Property list serialization — using NSPropertyListSerialization to convert between property lists (e.g., .plist files) and native Objective-C/Swift objects
XML serialization — using NSXMLParser or other libraries like SwiftyXML to convert between XML strings and native Objective-C/Swift objects
Core Data serialization — using Core Data's built-in serialization mechanisms to store and retrieve managed object contexts
Codable serialization — using Swift's Codable protocol to serialize and deserialize custom data types
Archiver/Unarchiver serialization — using NSKeyedArchiver and NSKeyedUnarchiver to serialize and deserialize objects that conform to the NSCoding protocol

knowledge.what_it_is: Networking is a fundamental concept in iOS development, enabling your app to communicate with servers and services over the internet. It involves understanding how to send and receive data using various protocols such as HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, and more.
what_you_will_learn: URLSession — Apple's recommended API for making network requests, providing a high-level interface for sending HTTP/HTTPS requests
URLRequest — represents a request to be sent over the network, including URL, method, headers, and body
HTTPMethod — understanding the different types of HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) and when to use each
URLSessionTask — a task that manages the lifecycle of a network request, providing callbacks for completion and error handling
Data — working with binary data in iOS, including encoding and decoding using Data, URL, and other APIs
JSONSerialization — converting JSON data to and from Foundation objects such as NSArray, NSDictionary, etc.
URLSessionDelegate — implementing the delegate methods to handle network request completion, failure, and cancellation
URLCache — caching frequently accessed resources to improve app performance and reduce network requests
URLProtocol — creating custom URL protocols for handling specific network requests or data formats

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) and HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) are communication protocols used to transfer data between a client (e.g., an iOS app) and a server. HTTP is used for standard web requests, while HTTPS uses encryption to secure the connection.
what_you_will_learn: URLs — Uniform Resource Locators: constructing URLs with schemes (http/https), hosts, paths, query parameters, and fragments
HTTP Methods — GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD: understanding when to use each method for data retrieval and modification
Request Headers — HTTP headers for authentication, caching, and content negotiation: Accept, Accept-Encoding, Authorization, Cache-Control, Content-Type, User-Agent
Response Status Codes — 1xx (Informational), 2xx (Successful), 3xx (Redirection), 4xx (Client Error), 5xx (Server Error): understanding common status codes for error handling and debugging
HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 — modern versions of the protocol with improved performance, multiplexing, and encryption: using Connection Coalescing, Header Compression, and QoS
TLS/SSL — Transport Layer Security/Secure Sockets Layer: configuring iOS apps to use HTTPS with certificates, keychains, and trust stores
NSURLSession — a high-level networking API for iOS: using shared containers, data tasks, upload/download tasks, and background sessions
URLSessionConfiguration — customizing URLSession behavior: setting cache policies, request timeout intervals, and credential storage

knowledge.what_it_is: REST (Representational State of Resource) is an architectural style for designing networked applications, commonly used in iOS app development to communicate with web servers and APIs. It emphasizes a client-server architecture, where the client makes requests to the server using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), and the server responds with the requested data.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP Methods — Understanding the different HTTP methods used in RESTful API interactions: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH
URL Encoding — Learning how to properly encode URLs for iOS app development using URL encoding schemes (e.g., percent-encoding)
Request Headers — Familiarizing yourself with common request headers used in RESTful APIs, such as Accept, Content-Type, and Authorization
JSON Data Format — Understanding the JSON data format and how it's used to represent data exchanged between iOS apps and web servers
Error Handling — Learning how to handle errors in RESTful API interactions, including error codes, HTTP status codes, and custom error responses
Caching Mechanisms — Familiarizing yourself with caching mechanisms, such as cache invalidation and cache expiration, to improve app performance
API Key Management — Understanding how to securely manage API keys for iOS apps, including key storage and rotation
iOS Networking Frameworks — Learning about popular iOS networking frameworks, such as Alamofire and URLSession, and their usage in RESTful API interactions

knowledge.what_it_is: GraphQL is a query language for APIs, developed by Facebook (Meta), that allows clients to request only the data they need, reducing network latency and improving app performance. It's widely adopted in iOS development for building scalable and efficient backend services.
what_you_will_learn: Schema — defining the structure of your GraphQL API using SDL (Schema Definition Language)
Query — writing client-side queries to fetch specific data from the server, including variables and arguments
Mutation — creating client-side mutations to modify data on the server, with support for optimistic updates
Resolver — implementing resolvers to handle incoming requests, perform business logic, and return data to clients
Apollo Client — using a popular GraphQL client library for iOS to simplify caching, pagination, and error handling
GraphQL Schema Stitching — combining multiple schemas into a single schema for complex backend services
Type System — understanding the type system in GraphQL, including scalar types (e.g., Int, String), object types, and interfaces

knowledge.what_it_is: Concurrency and multithreading are essential concepts in iOS development, allowing your app to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, improving responsiveness, and taking advantage of multi-core processors. This includes managing threads, synchronization primitives, and asynchronous programming.
what_you_will_learn: Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) — a high-level framework for concurrent execution, providing queues, serial and concurrent execution, and synchronization primitives
Dispatch Queues — creating and managing queues for concurrent execution, with options for serial, concurrent, or background execution
Serial Execution — executing tasks in a specific order, useful for tasks that require a linear sequence of operations
Concurrent Execution — executing multiple tasks simultaneously, improving responsiveness and throughput
Synchronization Primitives — using locks (NSLock, NSRecursiveLock), semaphores (dispatch_semaphore_create), and condition variables to coordinate access to shared resources
Operation Queues — managing concurrent execution of tasks with a high-level API, providing features like dependency management and cancellation
Async/Await — writing asynchronous code using the async/await syntax, making it easier to write concurrent code
Dispatch Groups — coordinating multiple tasks that need to complete before proceeding
Thread Safety — ensuring that shared resources are accessed safely by multiple threads

knowledge.what_it_is: Concurrency refers to the ability of an iOS app to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, improving responsiveness and user experience. This is achieved through various mechanisms such as threads, queues, and asynchronous programming.
what_you_will_learn: Threads — lightweight processes that can run concurrently with the main thread, using Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) or pthreads
Queues — data structures that manage a sequence of tasks to be executed in a specific order, including serial and concurrent queues
Dispatch Queues — high-level abstractions for managing threads and queues, providing a more efficient way to perform concurrent tasks
Operation Queues — a higher-level abstraction built on top of dispatch queues, allowing for easier management of asynchronous operations
Async/Await — a modern syntax for writing asynchronous code using promises and coroutines, improving readability and maintainability
Concurrency Patterns — design patterns that help manage concurrency, such as producer-consumer, pipeline, and parallelism

knowledge.what_it_is: Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) is a high-level framework introduced by Apple to simplify concurrency and multithreading on iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS. It provides a simple way to manage tasks asynchronously, improving app performance and responsiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Dispatch Queue — a container for executing tasks concurrently, with built-in support for serial and concurrent execution
Serial Dispatch Queue — a queue that executes tasks one by one, useful for tasks that require sequential execution
Concurrent Dispatch Queue — a queue that executes multiple tasks simultaneously, improving app performance
Dispatch Group — a way to manage groups of tasks, allowing for easier synchronization and cancellation
Dispatch Semaphore — a mechanism for limiting concurrent access to resources, preventing overloading
Dispatch Barrier — a way to synchronize tasks, ensuring that certain code is executed before others
Dispatch Work Item — the basic unit of work in GCD, representing a task to be executed asynchronously

knowledge.what_it_is: Operation queues are a high-level abstraction in iOS that manage and execute operations asynchronously, allowing developers to write more efficient and concurrent code.
what_you_will_learn: OperationQueue — a container for managing and executing operations, providing features like scheduling, prioritization, and cancellation
Operation — a unit of work that can be executed on an operation queue, with properties like qualityOfService, executionOrder, and dependencies
Serial vs Concurrent Queues — understanding the difference between serial and concurrent queues, including use cases for each
Scheduling Operations — learning how to schedule operations using addOperation, addOperations, and adding dependencies between operations
Cancelling Operations — understanding how to cancel operations using cancelAllOperations, cancelAllOperationsWithFinishBlock, and cancelling individual operations
Dependency Management — learning how to manage dependencies between operations using addDependency and removeDependency
Quality of Service (QoS) — understanding the different quality of service classes and their impact on operation execution
Operation Queue Priorities — learning how to set priorities for operation queues and operations, including use cases for high-priority operations

knowledge.what_it_is: The Swift Package Manager (SPM) is a dependency manager developed by Apple, integrated into Xcode since version 11. It allows developers to easily manage dependencies and create reusable packages.
what_you_will_learn: Package Structure — organizing code into targets, products, and sources

Targets — defining executable or library targets with specific settings and configurations
Products — specifying output files for each target, including frameworks and executables
Swift Packages — creating reusable packages that can be shared across projects
Package Publishers — publishing packages to the Swift Package Catalog or a custom repository
Package Consumers — using published packages in your own project, resolving dependencies at build time

knowledge.what_it_is: CocoaPods is a dependency manager for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS projects, allowing developers to easily integrate third-party libraries into their codebase.
what_you_will_learn: Podfile — the configuration file that lists dependencies and specifies build settings
Specs — pre-built frameworks containing library code, managed by CocoaPods
Target — a specific build configuration for a project or target
Integration — adding libraries to your project using pod install or pod update
Versioning — managing library versions in your Podfile and resolving conflicts

Spec repositories — remote repositories containing library specs, such as CocoaPods.org or a private repository

knowledge.what_it_is: Carthage is a dependency manager for Swift and Objective-C projects, similar to CocoaPods but with a more lightweight and flexible approach. It uses a Cartfile to declare dependencies and allows for easy switching between different versions of libraries.

Specs — defining custom specifications for Carthage to use when resolving dependencies
Binary Dependencies — downloading pre-built binary frameworks from the Carthage repository or a private server
Swift Packages — integrating Carthage with Swift Package Manager for seamless dependency management
Carthage Build Scripts — writing custom build scripts to automate tasks and workflows during the build process
Dependency Graphs — visualizing dependencies between libraries and projects using tools like graphviz
Library Versioning — managing different versions of libraries in a project, including pinning specific versions and using semantic versioning

knowledge.what_it_is: SwiftLint is a popular open-source tool for enforcing Swift coding standards and best practices, developed by the Realm team. It helps maintain code quality, consistency, and readability across large projects.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring SwiftLint rules — setting up custom configurations for specific projects or teams
Enforcing naming conventions — using SwiftLint to enforce consistent naming styles (e.g., camelCase, underscore notation)
Managing code style — configuring SwiftLint to enforce coding standards for indentation, spacing, and line length
Linter rules and severity levels — understanding the different types of linter rules (e.g., warning, error) and how to customize their severity
Integrating with Xcode and other IDEs — using SwiftLint's integration features to get real-time feedback within your preferred code editor
